text: How blessed are those whose actions are blameless, who obey the law of the Lord.

notes.text: Heb “[Oh] the happiness of those who are blameless of way.”
Heb “walk in.”

text: How blessed are those who observe his rules, and seek him with all their heart,
who, moreover, do no wrong, but follow in his footsteps.

notes.text: Heb “walk in his ways.”

text: You demand that your precepts be carefully kept.

notes.text: Heb “you, you commanded your precepts, to keep, very much.”

text: If only I were predisposed to keep your statutes!

notes.text: Heb “if only my ways were established.”

text: Then I would not be ashamed, if I were focused on all your commands.

notes.text: Or “when.”
Heb “I gaze at.”

text: I will give you sincere thanks, when I learn your just regulations.

notes.text: Heb “I will give you thanks with an upright heart.”

text: I will keep your statutes. Do not completely abandon me!

notes.text: Heb “do not abandon me to excess.” For other uses of the phrase עַד מְאֹד (’ad mÿ’od, “to excess”), see Ps 38:6, 8.

text: How can a young person maintain a pure life? By guarding it according to your instructions!

notes.text: Heb “young man.” Hebrew wisdom literature often assumes and reflects the male-oriented perspective of ancient Israelite society. The principle of the psalm is certainly applicable to all people, regardless of their gender or age. To facilitate modern application, the gender specific “young man” has been translated with the more neutral “young person.”
Heb “purify his path.”
Heb “by keeping according to your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX read the plural, “your words.”

text: With all my heart I seek you. Do not allow me to stray from your commands!
In my heart I store up your words, so I might not sin against you.

notes.text: Or “hide.”
Heb “your word.” Some medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX read the plural, “your words.”

text: You deserve praise, O! Teach me your statutes!

notes.text: Heb “[are] blessed.”

text: With my lips I proclaim all the regulations you have revealed.

notes.text: Heb “of your mouth.”

text: I rejoice in the lifestyle prescribed by your rules as if they were riches of all kinds.

notes.text: Heb “in the way of your rules.”
Heb “as upon,” meaning “as if” (see 2 Chr 32:19).
Heb “all wealth.” The phrase refers to all kinds of wealth and riches. See Prov 1:13; 6:31; 24:4; Ezek 27:12, 18.

text: I will meditate on your precepts and focus on your behavior.

notes.text: The cohortative verbal forms in this verse express the psalmist’s resolve.
Heb “gaze [at].”
Heb “ways” (referring figuratively to God’s behavior here).

text: I find delight in your statutes; I do not forget your instructions.

notes.text: The imperfects in this verse emphasize the attitude the psalmist maintains toward God’s law. Another option is to translate with the future tense, “I will find delight…I will not forget.”
Heb “your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX read the plural here.

text: Be kind to your servant! Then I will live and keep your instructions.

notes.text: The prefixed verbal form is probably a cohortative indicating purpose/result after the preceding imperative.
The cohortative with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the imperative that begins the verse.
Heb “your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss as well as several ancient versions read the plural here.

text: Open my eyes so I can truly see the marvelous things in your law!

notes.text: Heb “uncover.” The verb form גַּל (gal) is an apocopated Piel imperative from גָּלָה (galah, see GKC 214 §75.cc).
The cohortative with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

text: I am like a foreigner in this land. Do not hide your commands from me!

notes.text: Heb “I am a resident alien in the land.” Resident aliens were especially vulnerable and in need of help. They needed to know the social and legal customs of the land to avoid getting into trouble. The translation (note the addition of “like”) assumes the psalmist is speaking metaphorically, not literally.

text: I desperately long to know your regulations at all times.

notes.text: Heb “my soul languishes for longing for.”

text: You reprimand arrogant people. Those who stray from your commands are doomed.

notes.text: Heb “accursed.” The traditional punctuation of the Hebrew text takes “accursed” with the previous line (“arrogant, accursed ones”), but it is preferable to take it with the second line as the predicate of the statement.

text: Spare me shame and humiliation, for I observe your rules.

notes.text: Heb “roll away from upon me.” Some derive the imperatival form גַּל (gal) from גָּלָה (galah, “uncover,” as in v. 18), but here the form is from גָּלַל (galal, “roll”; see Josh 5:9, where חֶרְפָּה [kherpah, “shame; reproach”] also appears as object of the verb). Some, following the lead of a Dead Sea scroll (11QPsa), emend the form to גֹּל (gol).

text: Though rulers plot and slander me, your servant meditates on your statutes.

notes.text: Heb “though rulers sit, about me they talk together.” (For another example of the Niphal of דָּבַר (davar) used with a suffixed form of the preposition ב, see Ezek 33:30.)

text: Yes, I find delight in your rules; they give me guidance.

notes.text: Heb “men of my counsel.” That is, God’s rules are like advisers to the psalmist, for they teach him how to live in a godly manner that refutes the accusations of his enemies.

text: I collapse in the dirt. Revive me with your word!

notes.text: Heb “my soul clings to the dirt.” The Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, “being; soul”) with a pronominal suffix is often equivalent to a pronoun, especially in poetry (see BDB 660 s.v. נֶפֶשׁ 4.a).
Heb “according to your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural “your words.”

text: I told you about my ways and you answered me.

notes.text: Heb “my ways I proclaimed.”

text: Help me to understand what your precepts mean! Then I can meditate on your marvelous teachings.

notes.text: Heb “the way of your precepts make me understand.”
Heb “your amazing things,” which refers here to the teachings of the law (see v. 18).

text: I collapse from grief. Sustain me by your word!

notes.text: Some translate “my soul weeps,” taking the verb דָלַף (dalaf) from a root meaning “to drip; to drop” (BDB 196 s.v. דֶּלַף). On the basis of cognate evidence from Arabic and Akkadian, HALOT 223 s.v. II דלף proposes a homonymic root here, meaning “be sleepless.” Following L. C. Allen (Psalms 101-150 [WBC], 127, 135) the translation assumes that the verb is cognate with Ugaritic dlp, “to collapse; to crumple” in CTA 2 iv. 17, 26. See G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 44, 144.

text: Remove me from the path of deceit! Graciously give me your law!

notes.text: The “path of deceit” refers to a lifestyle characterized by deceit and disloyalty to God. It stands in contrast to the “way of faithfulness” in v. 30.
Heb “be gracious to me.” The verb is used metonymically here for “graciously giving” the law. (See Gen 33:5, where Jacob uses this verb in describing how God had graciously given him children.)

text: I choose the path of faithfulness; I am committed to your regulations.

notes.text: BDB 1000-1001 s.v. I שָׁוָה derives the verb from the first homonym listed, meaning “to agree with; to be like; to resemble.” It here means (in the Piel stem) “to be accounted suitable,” which in turn would mean by metonymy “to accept; to be committed to.” Some prefer to derive the verb from a homonym meaning “to place; to set,” but in this case an elliptical prepositional phrase must be understood, “I place your regulations [before me]” (see Ps 16:8).

text: I hold fast to your rules., do not let me be ashamed!

notes.text: Or “cling to.”

text: I run along the path of your commands, for you enable me to do so.

notes.text: Heb “for you make wide my heart.” The “heart” is viewed here as the seat of the psalmist’s volition and understanding. The Lord gives the psalmist the desire and moral understanding that are foundational to the willing obedience depicted metaphorically in the preceding line. In Isa 60:5 the expression “your heart will be wide” means “your heart will swell with pride,” but here the nuance appears to be different.

text: Teach me, O, the lifestyle prescribed by your statutes, so that I might observe it continually.

notes.text: Heb “the way of your statutes.”

Heb “and I will keep it to the end.” The prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative. The Hebrew term עֵקֶב (’eqev) is understood to mean “end” here. Another option is to take עֵקֶב (’eqev) as meaning “reward” here (see Ps 19:11) and to translate, “so that I might observe it and be rewarded.”

text: Give me understanding so that I might observe your law, and keep it with all my heart.

notes.text: The two prefixed verbal forms with vav (ו) conjunctive indicate purpose/result after the introductory imperative.

text: Guide me in the path of your commands, for I delight to walk in it.

notes.text: Or “make me walk.”
Heb “for in it I delight.”

text: Give me a desire for your rules, rather than for wealth gained unjustly.

notes.text: Heb “turn my heart to your rules.”
Heb “and not unjust gain.”

text: Turn my eyes away from what is worthless!

notes.text: Heb “Make my eyes pass by from looking at what is worthless.”
Heb “by your word.”

text: Confirm to your servant your promise, which you made to the one who honors you.

notes.text: Heb “word.”
Heb “which [is] for your fear,” that is, the promise made to those who exhibit fear of God.

text: Take away the insults that I dread! Indeed, your regulations are good.

notes.text: Heb “my reproach that I fear.”
Or “for.”

text: Look, I long for your precepts. Revive me with your deliverance!

notes.text: Or “righteousness.”

text: May I experience your loyal love,, and your deliverance, as you promised.

notes.text: Heb “and may your loyal love come to me.”
Or “salvation” (so many English versions).
Heb “according to your word.”

text: Then I will have a reply for the one who insults me, for I trust in your word.

notes.text: Heb “and I will answer [the] one who insults me a word.” The prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the jussive (see v. 41).

text: Do not completely deprive me of a truthful testimony, for I await your justice.

notes.text: Heb “do not snatch from my mouth a word of truth to excess.” The psalmist wants to be able to give a reliable testimony about the Lord’s loyal love (vv. 41-42), but if God does not intervene, the psalmist will be deprived of doing so, for the evidence of such love (i.e., deliverance) will be lacking.

text: Then I will keep your law continually now and for all time.

notes.text: The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the negated jussive (see v. 43).
Or “forever and ever.”

text: I will be secure, for I seek your precepts.

notes.text: Heb “and I will walk about in a wide place.” The cohortative with prefixed vav (ו) conjunctive gives a further consequence of the anticipated positive divine response (see vv. 43-44). Another option is to take the cohortative as expressing the psalmist’s request. In this case one could translate, “and please give me security.”

text: I will speak about your regulations before kings and not be ashamed.

notes.text: The series of four cohortatives with prefixed vav (ו) conjunctive in vv. 46-48 list further consequences of the anticipated positive divine response to the request made in v. 43.

text: I will find delight in your commands, which I love.
I will lift my hands to your commands, which I love, and I will meditate on your statutes.

notes.text: Lifting the hands is often associated with prayer (Pss 28:2; 63:4; Lam 2:19). (1) Because praying to God’s law borders on the extreme, some prefer to emend the text to “I lift up my hands to you,” eliminating “your commands, which I love” as dittographic. In this view these words were accidentally repeated from the previous verse. (2) However, it is possible that the psalmist closely associates the law with God himself because he views the law as the expression of the divine will. (3) Another option is that “lifting the hands” does not refer to prayer here, but to the psalmist’s desire to receive and appropriate the law. (4) Still others understand this to be an action praising God’s commands (so NCV; cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).

text: Remember your word to your servant, for you have given me hope.
This is what comforts me in my trouble, for your promise revives me.

notes.text: The demonstrative “this” refers back to the hope just mentioned or forward to the statement in the second line concerning the promise’s power to revive. See the note on the word “me” at the end of the verse for further discussion.

The hope generated by the promise (see v. 49b) brings comfort because (note “for” at the beginning of the line) the promise revives the psalmist’s spirits. Another option is to take כִּי (ki) at the beginning of the second line in the sense of “that,” in which case “this” refers to the promise’s power to revive.

text: Arrogant people do nothing but scoff at me. Yet I do not turn aside from your law.

notes.text: Heb “scoff at me to excess.”

text: I remember your ancient regulations,, and console myself.

notes.text: Heb “I remember your regulations from of old.” The prepositional phrase “from of old” apparently modifies “your regulations,” alluding to the fact that God revealed them to Israel in the distant past. Another option is to understand the prepositional phrase as modifying the verb, in which case one might translate, “I have long remembered your regulations.”
Or “find comfort.”

text: Rage takes hold of me because of the wicked, those who reject your law.
Your statutes have been my songs in the house where I live.

notes.text: Heb “songs were your statutes to me.”

Heb “in the house of my dwelling place.” Some take the Hebrew noun מָגוֹר (magor) in the sense of “temporary abode,” and see this as a reference to the psalmist’s status as a resident alien (see v. 19). But the noun can refer to a dwelling place in general (see Ps 55:15).

text: I remember your name during the night, O and I will keep your law.

notes.text: The cohortative verbal form expresses the psalmist’s resolve to obey the law.

text: has been my practice, for I observe your precepts.

notes.text: Heb “this has been to me.” The demonstrative “this” (1) refers back to the practices mentioned in vv. 54-55, or (2) looks forward to the statement in the second line, in which case the כִּי (ki) at the beginning of the second line should be translated “that.”

text: The is my source of security. I have determined to follow your instructions.

notes.text: Heb “my portion [is] the Lord.” The psalmist compares the Lord to landed property, which was foundational to economic stability in ancient Israel (see Ps 16:5).
Heb “I said.”
Heb “to keep your words” (see v. 9).

text: I seek your favor with all my heart. Have mercy on me as you promised!

notes.text: Heb “I appease your face.”

text: I consider my actions and follow your rules.

notes.text: Heb “my ways.”
Heb “and I turn my feet toward.”

text: I keep your commands eagerly and without delay.

notes.text: Heb “I hurry and I do not delay to keep your commands.”

text: The ropes of the wicked tighten around me, but I do not forget your law.

notes.text: Heb “surround.”

text: In the middle of the night I arise to thank you for your just regulations.

notes.text: The psalmist uses an imperfect verbal form to emphasize that this is his continuing practice.

text: I am a friend to all your loyal followers, and to those who keep your precepts.

notes.text: Heb “to all who fear you.”

text: , your loyal love fills the earth.
You are good to your servant,, just as you promised.

notes.text: Heb “do good.”

text: Teach me proper discernment and understanding! For I consider your commands to be reliable.

notes.text: Heb “goodness of taste.” Here “taste” refers to moral and ethical discernment.
Heb “for I believe in your commands.”

text: Before I was afflicted I used to stray off, but now I keep your instructions.

notes.text: Heb “before I suffered, I was straying off.”
Heb “your word.”

text: You are good and you do good.
Arrogant people smear my reputation with lies, but I observe your precepts with all my heart.

notes.text: Heb “smear over me a lie.”

text: Their hearts are calloused, but I find delight in your law.

notes.text: Heb “their heart is insensitive like fat.”

text: It was good for me to suffer, so that I might learn your statutes.
The law you have revealed is more important to me than thousands of pieces of gold and silver.

notes.text: Heb “better to me [is] the law of your mouth than thousands of gold and silver.”

text: Your hands made me and formed me. Give me understanding so that I might learn your commands.

notes.text: Heb “made me and established me.” The two verbs also appear together in Deut 32:6, where God, compared to a father, is said to have “made and established” Israel.
The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

text: Your loyal followers will be glad when they see me, for I find hope in your word.

notes.text: Heb “those who fear you will see me and rejoice.”

text: I know,, that your regulations are just. You disciplined me because of your faithful devotion to me.

notes.text: In this context (note the second line) the Hebrew term מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim), which so often refers to the regulations of God’s law elsewhere in this psalm, may refer instead to his decisions or disciplinary judgment.
Heb “and [in] faithfulness you afflicted me.”

text: May your loyal love console me, as you promised your servant.

notes.text: Heb “according to your word to your servant.”

text: May I experience your compassion, so I might live! For I find delight in your law.

notes.text: Heb “and may your compassion come to me.”

text: May the arrogant be humiliated, for they have slandered me! But I meditate on your precepts.

notes.text: Heb “for [with] falsehood they have denied me justice.”

text: May your loyal followers turn to me, those who know your rules.

notes.text: Heb “those who fear you.”

text: May I be fully committed to your statutes, so that I might not be ashamed.

notes.text: Heb “may my heart be complete in your statutes.”

text: I desperately long for your deliverance. I find hope in your word.

notes.text: Heb “my soul pines for.” See Ps 84:2.

text: My eyes grow tired as I wait for your promise to be fulfilled. I say, “When will you comfort me?”

notes.text: Heb “my eyes fail for your word.” The psalmist has intently kept his eyes open, looking for God to intervene, but now his eyes are watery and bloodshot, impairing his vision. See Ps 69:3.
Heb “saying.”

text: For I am like a wineskin dried up in smoke. I do not forget your statutes.

notes.text: Or “even though.”
The Hebrew word נֹאד (no’d, “leather container”) refers to a container made from animal skin which is used to hold wine or milk (see Josh 9:4, 13; Judg 4:19; 1 Sam 16:20).
Heb “in the smoke.”

text: How long must your servant endure this? When will you judge those who pursue me?

notes.text: Heb “How long are the days of your servant?”

text: The arrogant dig pits to trap me, which violates your law.

notes.text: Heb “for me.”
Heb “which [is] not according to your law.”

text: All your commands are reliable. I am pursued without reason. Help me!

notes.text: God’s commands are a reliable guide to right and wrong. By keeping them the psalmist is doing what is right, yet he is still persecuted.

text: They have almost destroyed me here on the earth, but I do not reject your precepts.
Revive me with your loyal love, that I might keep the rules you have revealed.

notes.text: Heb “according to.”

text: , your instructions endure; they stand secure in heaven.

notes.text: Heb “Forever, O Lord, your word stands firm in heaven,” or “Forever, O Lord, [is] your word; it stands firm in heaven.” The translation assumes that “your word” refers here to the body of divine instructions contained in the law (note the frequent references to the law in vv. 92-96). See vv. 9, 16-17, 57, 101, 105, 130, 139 and 160-61. The reference in v. 86 to God’s law being faithful favors this interpretation. Another option is that “your word” refers to God’s assuring word of promise, mentioned in vv. 25, 28, 42, 65, 74, 81, 107, 114, 147 and 169. In this case one might translate, “O Lord, your promise is reliable, it stands firm in heaven.”

text: You demonstrate your faithfulness to all generations. You established the earth and it stood firm.

notes.text: Heb “to a generation and a generation [is] your faithfulness.”

text: Today they stand firm by your decrees, for all things are your servants.
If I had not found encouragement in your law, I would have died in my sorrow.

notes.text: Heb “if your law had not been my delight.”
Or “my suffering.”

text: I will never forget your precepts, for by them you have revived me.
I belong to you. Deliver me! For I seek your precepts.
The wicked prepare to kill me, yet I concentrate on your rules.

notes.text: Heb “the wicked wait for me to kill me.”

text: I realize that everything has its limits, but your commands are beyond full comprehension.

notes.text: Heb “to every perfection I have seen an end, your command is very wide.” God’s law is beyond full comprehension, which is why the psalmist continually studies it (vv. 95, 97).

text: O how I love your law! All day long I meditate on it.
Your commandments make me wiser than my enemies, for I am always aware of them.

notes.text: The plural form needs to be revocalized as a singular in order to agree with the preceding singular verb and the singular pronoun in the next line. The Lord’s “command” refers here to the law (see Ps 19:8).

text: I have more insight than all my teachers, for I meditate on your rules.
I am more discerning than those older than I,
I stay away from the evil path, so that I might keep your instructions.

notes.text: Heb “I hold back my feet.”
Heb “your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural.

text: I do not turn aside from your regulations, for you teach me.
Your words are sweeter in my mouth than honey!

notes.text: Heb “How smooth they are to my palate, your word, more than honey to my mouth.” A few medieval Hebrew mss, as well as several other ancient witnesses, read the plural “your words,” which can then be understood as the subject of the plural verb “they are smooth.”

text: Your precepts give me discernment. Therefore I hate all deceitful actions.

notes.text: Heb “every false path.”

text: Your word is a lamp to walk by, and a light to illumine my path.

notes.text: Many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural (“words”).
Heb “[is] a lamp for my foot and a light for my path.”

text: I have vowed and solemnly sworn to keep your just regulations.
I am suffering terribly., revive me with your word!
, please accept the freewill offerings of my praise! Teach me your regulations!

notes.text: Heb “of my mouth.”

text: My life is in continual danger,

notes.text: Heb “my life [is] in my hands continually.”

text: The wicked lay a trap for me, but I do not wander from your precepts.
I claim your rules as my permanent possession, for they give me joy.

notes.text: Heb “for the joy of my heart [are] they.”

text: I am determined to obey your statutes at all times, to the very end.

notes.text: Heb “I turn my heart to do.”

text: I hate people with divided loyalties, but I love your law.

notes.text: Heb “divided ones.” The word occurs only here; it appears to be derived from a verbal root, attested in Arabic, meaning “to split” (see HALOT 762 s.v. *סֵעֵף). Since the psalmist is emphasizing his unswerving allegiance to God and his law, the term probably refers to those who lack such loyalty. The translation is similar to that suggested by L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 131.

text: You are my hiding place and my shield.
Turn away from me, you evil men, so that I can observe the commands of my God.

notes.text: The psalmist has already declared that he observes God’s commands despite persecution, so here the idea must be “so that I might observe the commands of my God unhindered by threats.”

text: Sustain me as you promised, so that I will live. Do not disappoint me!

notes.text: The prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.
Heb “do not make me ashamed of my hope.” After the Hebrew verb בּוֹשׁ (bosh, “to be ashamed”) the preposition מִן (min, “from”) often introduces the reason for shame.

text: Support me, so that I will be delivered. Then I will focus on your statutes continually.

notes.text: Or “and that I might focus.” The two cohortatives with vav (ו) conjunctive indicate purpose/result after the imperative at the beginning of the verse.

text: You despise all who stray from your statutes, for they are deceptive and unreliable.

notes.text: The Hebrew verb סָלָה (salah, “to disdain”) occurs only here and in Lam 1:15. Cognate usage in Aramaic and Akkadian, as well as Lam 1:15, suggest it may have a concrete nuance of “to throw away.”
Heb “for their deceit [is] falsehood.”

text: You remove all the wicked of the earth like slag. Therefore I love your rules.

notes.text: Traditionally “dross” (so KJV, ASV, NIV). The metaphor comes from metallurgy; “slag” is the substance left over after the metallic ore has been refined.
As he explains in the next verse, the psalmist’s fear of judgment motivates him to obey God’s rules.

text: My body trembles because I fear you; I am afraid of your judgments.

notes.text: Heb “my flesh.”
The Hebrew verb סָמַר (samar, “to tremble”) occurs only here and in Job 4:15.
Heb “from fear of you.” The pronominal suffix on the noun is an objective genitive.

text: I do what is fair and right. Do not abandon me to my oppressors!

notes.text: Heb “do justice and righteousness.”

text: Guarantee the welfare of your servant! Do not let the arrogant oppress me!

notes.text: Heb “be surety for your servant for good.”

text: My eyes grow tired as I wait for your deliverance, for your reliable promise to be fulfilled.

notes.text: Heb “my eyes fail for your deliverance.” The psalmist has intently kept his eyes open, looking for God to intervene, but now his eyes are watery and bloodshot, impairing his vision. See the similar phrase in v. 82.
Heb “and for the word of your faithfulness.”

text: Show your servant your loyal love!

notes.text: Heb “do with your servant according to your loyal love.”

text: I am your servant. Give me insight, so that I can understand

notes.text: or “know.” The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

text: It is time for the to act – they break your law!
For this reason I love your commands more than gold, even purest gold.

notes.text: “For this reason” connects logically with the statement made in v. 126. Because the judgment the psalmist fears (see vv. 119-120) is imminent, he remains loyal to God’s law.

text: For this reason I carefully follow all your precepts. I hate all deceitful actions.

notes.text: Heb “for this reason all the precepts of everything I regard as right.” The phrase “precepts of everything” is odd. It is preferable to take the kaf (כ) on כֹּל (kol, “everything) with the preceding form as a pronominal suffix, “your precepts,” and the lamed (ל) with the following verb as an emphatic particle. See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 138.

text: Your rules are marvelous. Therefore I observe them.
Your instructions are a doorway through which light shines. They give insight to the untrained.

notes.text: Heb “the doorway of your words gives light.” God’s “words” refer here to the instructions in his law (see vv. 9, 57).
Heb “it [i.e., the doorway] gives.”
Or “the [morally] naive,” that is, the one who is young and still in the process of learning right from wrong and distinguishing wisdom from folly. See Pss 19:7; 116:6.

text: I open my mouth and pant, because I long for your commands.

notes.text: The verb occurs only here in the OT.

text: Turn toward me and extend mercy to me, as you typically do to your loyal followers.

notes.text: Heb “according to custom toward the lovers of your name.” The “lovers of” God’s “name” are the Lord’s loyal followers. See Pss 5:11; 69:36; Isa 56:6.

text: Direct my steps by your word! Do not let any sin dominate me!

notes.text: God’s “word” refers here to his law (see v. 11).

text: Deliver me from oppressive men, so that I can keep your precepts.

notes.text: Or “redeem me.”

text: Smile on your servant!

notes.text: Heb “cause your face to shine.”

text: Tears stream down from my eyes, because people do not keep your law.

notes.text: Heb “[with] flowing streams my eyes go down.”
Heb “they”; even though somewhat generic, the referent (people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

text: You are just, O and your judgments are fair.
The rules you impose are just, and absolutely reliable.

notes.text: Heb “you commanded [in] justice your rules.”

text: My zeal consumes for my enemies forget your instructions.

notes.text: or “zeal.”
Heb “destroys,” in a hyperbolic sense.
Heb “your words.”

text: Your word is absolutely pure, and your servant loves it!
I am insignificant and despised, yet I do not forget your precepts.
Your justice endures, and your law is reliable.

notes.text: Heb “your justice [is] justice forever.”
Or “truth.”

text: Distress and hardship confront yet I find delight in your commands.

notes.text: Heb “find.”

text: Your rules remain just. Give me insight so that I can live.

notes.text: Heb “just are your rules forever.”

text: I cried out with all my heart, “Answer me, O I will observe your statutes.”
I cried out to you, “Deliver me, your rules.”
I am up before dawn crying for help.
My eyes anticipate the nighttime hours, so that I can meditate on your word.
Listen to me because of your loyal love!, revive me, as you typically do!

notes.text: Heb “my voice.”
Heb “according to your custom.”

text: Those who are eager to do wrong draw near; they are far from your law.

notes.text: Heb “those who pursue.”

text: You are near, O and all your commands are reliable.
I learned long ago that you ordained your rules to last.

notes.text: Heb “long ago I knew concerning your rules, that forever you established them.” See v. 89 for the same idea. The translation assumes that the preposition מִן (min) prefixed to “your rules” introduces the object of the verb יָדַע (yada’), as in 1 Sam 23:23. Another option is that the preposition indicates source, in which case one might translate, “Long ago I realized from your rules that forever you established them” (cf. NIV, NRSV).

text: See my pain and rescue me! For I do not forget your law.
Fight for me and defend me!

notes.text: Or “argue my case.”
Heb “and redeem me.” The verb “redeem” casts the Lord in the role of a leader who protects members of his extended family in times of need and crisis (see Ps 19:14).

text: The wicked have no chance for deliverance, for they do not seek your statutes.

notes.text: Heb “far from the wicked [is] deliverance.”

text: Your compassion is great, O Revive me, as you typically do!

notes.text: Heb “according to your customs.”

text: The enemies who chase me are numerous. Yet I do not turn aside from your rules.

notes.text: Heb “many [are] those who chase me and my enemies.”

text: I take note of the treacherous and despise them, because they do not keep your instructions.
See how I love your precepts!, revive me with your loyal love!
Your instructions are totally reliable; all your just regulations endure.

notes.text: Heb “the head of your word is truth, and forever [is] all your just regulation.” The term “head” is used here of the “sum total” of God’s instructions.

text: Rulers pursue me for no reason, yet I am more afraid of disobeying your instructions.

notes.text: Heb “and because of your instructions my heart trembles.” The psalmist’s healthy “fear” of the consequences of violating God’s instructions motivates him to obey them. See v. 120.

text: I rejoice in your instructions, like one who finds much plunder.

notes.text: Heb “like one who finds great plunder.” See Judg 5:30. The image is that of a victorious warrior who finds a large amount of plunder on the field of battle.

text: I hate and despise deceit; I love your law.
Seven times a day I praise you because of your just regulations.

notes.text: The number “seven” is use rhetorically to suggest thoroughness.

text: Those who love your law are completely secure; nothing causes them to stumble.

notes.text: Heb “great peace [is] to the lovers of your law.”
Heb “and there is no stumbling to them.”

text: I hope for your deliverance, O and I obey

notes.text: Heb “do.”

text: I keep your rules; I love them greatly.
I keep your precepts and rules, for you are aware of everything I do.

notes.text: Heb “for all my ways [are] before you.”

text: Listen to my cry for help, Give me insight by your word!

notes.text: Heb “may my cry approach before you.”

text: Listen to my appeal for mercy! Deliver me, as you promised.

notes.text: Heb “may my appeal for mercy come before you.”
Heb “according to your speech.”

text: May praise flow freely from my lips, for you teach me your statutes.
May my tongue sing about your instructions, for all your commands are just.
May your hand help me, for I choose to obey

notes.text: The words “to obey” are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and for clarity.

text: I long for your deliverance, O; I find delight in your law.
May I live and praise you! May your regulations help me!

notes.text: Heb “my life.”
God’s regulations will “help” the psalmist by giving him moral and ethical guidance.

text: I have wandered off like a lost sheep. Come looking for your servant, for I do not forget your commands.

notes.text: Heb “I stray like a lost sheep.” It is possible that the point of the metaphor is vulnerability: The psalmist, who is threatened by his enemies, feels as vulnerable as a straying, lost sheep. This would not suggest, however, that he has wandered from God’s path (see the second half of the verse, as well as v. 110).
